The FARs.clue: This is the 14 CFR part that outlines the performance rules for aircraft maintenance.
choices: Part 43
Part 21
Part 91
Part 147
The FARs.explanation: Part 43 contains the performance rules for maintenance, preventive maintenance, rebuilding, and alteration. Part 21 covers certification procedures for products and articles. Part 91 covers general operating rules. Part 147 covers aviation maintenance technician schools.

The FARs.clue: According to 14 CFR Part 43, this statement about preventive maintenance is correct.
choices: Preventive maintenance includes any repair as long as the technician deems it minor
Only A&P mechanics may perform preventive maintenance
Preventive maintenance consists of simple or minor preservation operations listed in Part 43 Appendix A
Preventive maintenance requires a Designated Engineering Representative's signature
The FARs.explanation: Preventive maintenance is specifically defined by the list in Part 43 Appendix A. A certificated pilot (at least Private) may perform preventive maintenance on their own aircraft under the conditions stated in Part 43. It is not 'anything minor' by technician judgment, and it does not require DER involvement.

The FARs.clue: According to 14 CFR Part 43 Appendix A, this describes a major alteration.
choices: Any task that takes more than one technician to complete
Replacement of any part, regardless of impact
A change to type-design items that might appreciably affect weight
An alteration that costs more than a specified dollar threshold
The FARs.explanation: Appendix A classifies alterations as major based on the potential effect on airworthiness-related qualities, not labor, cost, or any-part replacement. Major alterations require approved data and documentation on Form 337.

The FARs.clue: This is who is authorized to perform pitot-static and transponder inspections under 14 CFR 91.411 and 91.413.
choices: Any certificated pilot who owns the aircraft
A certificated repair station with the appropriate ratings
Any A&P mechanic with general maintenance privileges
No one — only the FAA performs these inspections
The FARs.explanation: 91.411 and 91.413 require appropriately-rated repair stations or the manufacturer. General pilot or A&P privileges do not extend to the 24-month certifications for altimeter/pitot-static and transponder systems. The FAA does not perform maintenance inspections.

The FARs.clue: According to FAA regulations, this is the definition of 'maintenance.'
choices: Any activity that keeps an aircraft operating
Inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement
Only replacing failed parts
Any work performed by an A&P mechanic
The FARs.explanation: The Part 1 definition is specific: maintenance = inspection, overhaul, repair, preservation, and parts replacement — but NOT preventive maintenance, which is defined separately. General phrases like 'any activity' or 'any work by an A&P' are not the regulatory definition.

Records & Forms.clue: Per 14 CFR §43.9, these four elements must be in every maintenance record entry.
choices: Only the date and the technician's initials
Description of work, date of completion, name of person, and approving signature
The number of hours spent on the work
The customer's name and address
Records & Forms.explanation: Section 43.9 specifies the four required elements. Time spent and customer contact information are not required by §43.9 (though they may be recorded for business purposes).

Records & Forms.clue: This is how an altimeter or transponder test must be documented in the maintenance records.
choices: With just a sticker on the instrument
Verbally confirmed with the pilot
Only by the flight crew in the aircraft discrepancy log
With the date of the test, the maximum altitude tested, and the approving signature
Records & Forms.explanation: Proper documentation includes the test date, maximum altitude (altimeter only), and the signature/certificate data of the person approving the return to service. Stickers alone are informational but do not satisfy the record-keeping requirement. Verbal confirmation and pilot-side logs do not substitute for the maintenance record entry.

Records & Forms.clue: This is what the acronym STC stands for.
choices: Standard Technical Certificate
Specialized Tool Certification
Standard Testing Code
Supplemental Type Certificate
Records & Forms.explanation: STC is Supplemental Type Certificate — the FAA's approval for a major design change to a type-certificated product.

Records & Forms.clue: This is the primary purpose of a Part 145 repair station's capability list.
choices: To identify the station's employee roster
To identify the specific articles (by make, model, or description) the station
To list the station's pricing for each job
To record the aircraft the station has worked on historically
Records & Forms.explanation: The capability list identifies what the station is certified to work on — by make/model or description. Employee rosters, pricing, and work history are separate records that do not constitute the capability list.

Records & Forms.clue: This is the primary purpose of FAA Form 8130-3 (Authorized Release Certificate / Airworthiness Approval Tag).
choices: To approve new or overhauled aircraft parts for return to service
To record a pilot's flight review
To document an aircraft's registration status
To serve as a sales invoice for aircraft parts
Records & Forms.explanation: The 8130-3 is an airworthiness-approval and traceability document for aircraft parts. It is not related to pilot flight reviews, aircraft registration, or sales invoicing (though the tag often accompanies sales of new or overhauled parts).

Repair Station Ops.clue: According to most Part 145 training programs, this is the typical interval for recurrent training of repair station personnel.
choices: Not exceeding every 6 months
Not exceeding every 60 months
Only upon hire — no recurrent training required
Not exceeding every 24 months
Repair Station Ops.explanation: A 24-month cycle is typical for Part 145 recurrent training. Shorter (6-month) intervals are uncommon. 60 months is too long to keep personnel current. Recurrent training is required — initial training alone does not satisfy Part 145 requirements.

Repair Station Ops.clue: Under Part 145, this party is responsible for ensuring that current technical data is available and used during avionics maintenance.
choices: The repair station itself
The individual aircraft owner
The FAA
The part manufacturer alone
Repair Station Ops.explanation: Part 145 places the responsibility on the repair station. The station's accepted procedures manual designates specific personnel for this responsibility. The FAA oversees; it does not maintain individual stations' technical data. Part manufacturers publish the data but do not ensure it is in use at a specific station.

Repair Station Ops.clue: This is why a Part 145 repair station must keep walkways and work areas free of clutter.
choices: For visual appeal during FAA inspections only
Because Part 145 has no such requirement
To comply with OSHA but not Part 145
To reduce safety hazards
Repair Station Ops.explanation: Housekeeping addresses both safety and quality. Part 145 does include housekeeping-related requirements, and OSHA adds workplace safety requirements — the two are complementary, not alternatives. Visual appeal during inspections is a side effect, not the primary reason.

Repair Station Ops.clue: If a Part 145 station's FAA-approved antidrug program includes pre-employment drug testing, this is required of a new technician.
choices: Complete and pass the pre-employment drug test before starting work
Ignore the test if they feel confident they would pass
Complete the test at any time during the first year of employment
Self-certify on an honor system
Repair Station Ops.explanation: Pre-employment testing must be completed and passed BEFORE starting work in a safety-sensitive position. It cannot be deferred, ignored, or self-certified. Part 120 sets the federal requirements; Part 145 stations incorporate them into their programs.

Repair Station Ops.clue: Before using any specialized test equipment in a Part 145 environment, this must be confirmed.
choices: The technician has used the equipment before
The equipment is the latest model
The equipment was purchased within the last year
The equipment's calibration is current and traceable
Repair Station Ops.explanation: Calibration currency and traceability are the regulatory requirements. Familiarity, model age, or recent purchase do not substitute for verified calibration. Out-of-calibration equipment cannot be used for maintenance decisions.

Approvals & Alterations.clue: If a TSO part has an STC for installation AND the STC includes a flight manual supplement, this describes the installation.
choices: A minor alteration that requires only a logbook entry
Preventive maintenance that a pilot may perform
An operation requiring a DER field approval with no Form 337
A major alteration requiring approved data (the STC) and typically FAA Form
Approvals & Alterations.explanation: The presence of an STC with a flight manual supplement strongly indicates a major alteration — the installation affects operating procedures or performance enough to warrant an AFM supplement. Approved data (the STC) is used, and Form 337 is typically required.

Approvals & Alterations.clue: If a TSO part is replaced with another TSO part of equivalent form, fit, and function, this is how the installation is generally classified.
choices: Generally NOT a major alteration
A major alteration requiring Form 337
An alteration requiring an STC
Preventive maintenance that a pilot-owner may perform
Approvals & Alterations.explanation: Like-for-like TSO replacement is maintenance, not a major alteration. A logbook entry suffices. An STC is not needed for an equivalent TSO part. Pilot-owner preventive maintenance is a separate narrow category not including general avionics replacement.

Approvals & Alterations.clue: For intermittent VOR NAV flag indications, this is a useful first step a Part 145 technician can take.
choices: Immediately replace the VOR receiver
Only write up the squawk and return the aircraft to service
Rewrite the aircraft logbook
Verify physical-layer items first: antenna condition and mounting
Approvals & Alterations.explanation: Physical-layer checks are the first step for intermittent symptoms. Receiver replacement is expensive and often unnecessary. A write-up with no action is not a resolution. Logbook rewriting is not appropriate.

Approvals & Alterations.clue: This is the FAA-issued one-time approval for a major alteration or repair that has no existing approved data.
choices: Field Approval
Supplemental Type Certificate
Technical Standard Order
Parts Manufacturer Approval
Approvals & Alterations.explanation: A Field Approval is an FAA Inspector's one-time authorization of a major alteration or repair, documented on Form 337. STC is a pre-engineered design approval for production use. TSO certifies a part meets a performance standard. PMA allows production of replacement parts.

Approvals & Alterations.clue: This FAA document legally mandates corrective action on an unsafe condition and is enforceable on aircraft operators.
choices: Service Bulletin
Service Letter
Maintenance Alert
Airworthiness Directive
Approvals & Alterations.explanation: An Airworthiness Directive (AD) is a legally binding FAA order issued when an unsafe condition exists. Manufacturer Service Bulletins are advisory unless an AD references them. Service Letters and Maintenance Alerts are informational only.

Extended Coverage.clue: This governs whether work may start when an article sits on the capability list but the newest manual revision is not yet in the shop.
choices: The customer may sign a waiver and work proceeds
Work may not begin until current technical data is on hand
The capability list alone authorizes the work
The FAA inspector must approve the missing revision
Extended Coverage.explanation: Part 145 Section 145.109 requires a repair station to have and use the current manufacturer maintenance manual or other FAA-approved data. Capability list coverage is not a substitute for current data. A customer waiver and verbal FAA approval cannot replace the regulation.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: A repair station’s capability list shows a certain radio as “within capabilities,” but the latest manual revision is not available. Can you perform maintenance on it?

Extended Coverage.clue: This FAA advisory circular is the default acceptable-methods reference for avionics wiring installation workmanship in a Part 145 shop.
Extended Coverage.explanation: AC 43.13-1B, especially Chapter 11, gives the FAA's accepted methods, techniques, and practices for aircraft electrical wiring installation. AC 20-138 addresses GPS/GNSS installations, AC 23-17 deals with Part 23 systems, and AC 90-100 covers U.S. RNAV operations.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: When installing avionics wiring in a Part 145 shop, which standard is most crucial?

Extended Coverage.clue: This is the proper description of a Part 145 repair station's capability list.
choices: An FAA-approved substitute for manufacturer manuals
A list of aircraft types the station may return to service
A record of every tool the station owns
A list of specific articles the station may maintain within its approved ratings
Extended Coverage.explanation: Section 145.215 defines the capability list as identifying the specific articles, within the shop's ratings, that it is authorized to maintain. It is not a manual substitute, nor an airframe privilege, nor a tool inventory.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: Which statement best describes the scope of a repair station’s capabilities under Part 145?

Extended Coverage.clue: This is the first reasonable action for a new technician troubleshooting an intermittent VOR NAV flag complaint in a Part 145 shop.
choices: Swap the antenna and coax without testing
Replace the VOR receiver on the bench
Verify the complaint and review the manufacturer troubleshooting procedures
Return the unit to service with a pilot placard
Extended Coverage.explanation: Troubleshooting begins with verifying the reported symptom and consulting the approved manufacturer data. Swapping parts before confirming the problem wastes time and may mask the true cause. Placarding does not address airworthiness of a required NAV system.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: A pilot complains of intermittent NAV flag indications on a VOR receiver. Which step might a new technician take first in a Part 145 shop?

Extended Coverage.clue: This combination of details must appear in the return-to-service log entry for an avionics maintenance task under Section 43.9.
choices: Only the customer's name and invoice number
A photograph of the removed component
Description of work, date completed, technician signature, and certificate number
The aircraft sale history
Extended Coverage.explanation: Section 43.9 requires each maintenance record entry to include a description of the work performed, the date of completion, and the signature and certificate number of the person approving the work. Invoices, photos, or ownership history are not regulatory substitutes.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: What must be included in a maintenance log entry for an avionics repair?

Extended Coverage.clue: This is the recommended technique for inserting or extracting terminated contacts in a circular connector.
choices: The correct insertion/extraction tool sized for that connector series
Needle-nose pliers on the contact barrel
Pulling directly on the wire
A heated pick to soften the grommet
Extended Coverage.explanation: Circular connectors use retention clips and rubber grommets that are easily damaged. The correct, color-coded insertion/extraction tool for the connector series seats and releases contacts without harming the grommet or clip. The other methods routinely cause connector damage.
Extended Coverage.targetCaetStem: When inserting or extracting terminated wires from a standard circular connector, which practice is recommended in a Part 145 shop?

final.clue: Under 14 CFR Part 43, this specific document must be completed and submitted to the FAA within 48 hours after a major repair or major alteration.
choices: Form 337
Form 8130-3
Form 8110-3
Logbook Entry
final.explanation: FAA Form 337 (Major Repair and Alteration) is required to document major repairs/alterations and must be forwarded to the FAA within 48 hours of returning the aircraft to service.

final.clue: A Part 145 Repair Station cannot perform alterations on an aircraft without approved data. Which of the following is NOT considered 'approved data' by the FAA?
choices: Type Certificate Data Sheet (TCDS)
Airworthiness Directives (ADs)
Advisory Circular 43.13-1B
Supplemental Type Certificate (STC)
final.explanation: AC 43.13-1B is 'acceptable data', not 'approved data' unless specifically approved by an FAA inspector for a major alteration. TCDS, ADs, and STCs are approved data.

final.clue: When approving an aircraft for return to service after a 100-hour inspection, the maintenance record entry must include this specific statement.
choices: Certified airworthy for immediate flight
I certify that this aircraft has been inspected in
Inspected and cleared per 14 CFR Part 91
Operations check satisfactory
final.explanation: 14 CFR 43.11 dictates the exact wording required for an inspection return to service: 'I certify that this aircraft has been inspected in accordance with... and was determined to be in airworthy condition.'
